description: If a relation works both ways, then swapping the arguments is fair game.
goal: (-> (forall x (forall y (-> (App (App R x) y) (App (App R y) x)))) (-> (App (App R a) b) (App (App R b) a)))

guides.text: A **symmetric** relation is one where $R(x,y)$ always implies $R(y,x)$. For example, 'is a sibling of' is symmetric: if Alice is a sibling of Bob, then Bob is a sibling of Alice. The goal says: if $R$ is symmetric, then from $R(a,b)$ we can conclude $R(b,a)$. This is a two-step instantiation of the symmetry property.
Assume $R$ is symmetric: for all $x$ and $y$, $R(x,y)$ [implication|implies] $R(y,x)$. This is the [antecedent|antecedent] of the main implication.
guides.tactic: (assume (forall x (forall y (-> (App (App R x) y) (App (App R y) x)))))

guides.text: Now assume the second [antecedent|antecedent]: $R(a,b)$. We need to reach $R(b,a)$.
Instantiate the symmetry property with $a$, replacing the outer $x$. This gives $\forall y.\; R(a,y) \to R(y,a)$ — 'if $R$ relates $a$ to something, that something relates back to $a$.'
Now instantiate with $b$, replacing $y$. We get $R(a,b) \to R(b,a)$ — exactly the [implication|implication] we need.
[discharge|Discharge] the symmetry premise to complete the proof.
guides.tactic: (->-intro (forall x (forall y (-> (App (App R x) y) (App (App R y) x)))) 6)
